Guys, JB's in the house. Pop your daily motivation slash skills training and today we are focusing on hard core skills but maybe a touch of motivation here. So what I want to talk about today is lining up the five core elements of the straight line system. What that really means, how you do it. I'm sure by now you should have a basic understanding. You should have more than that even. But I want to really dig into this today and give you a quick tutorial and show you what it means and how logical it really is from a different perspective. Let me explain what I mean. So what are the five core elements? Let's start with that. What are they? Simple, right? Number one. So the five core elements, what it means is that these five elements must all fall into place for someone to say yes. If any one of these five elements is not lined up correctly, it's almost impossible to close the sale. Very, very difficult to close a sale. The best metaphor I can use for this is what a safe cracker does. How a safe cracker goes about cracking a safe. So let's first talk about what the numbers are in the combination. Those are your five core elements, all your five numbers of a combination line. So imagine if the human brain, someone's buying strategy, how they go about, so buying strategies, how they go about getting people to say yes, I'll do it. That's someone's buying strategy. What goes on? So for someone to say yes, imagine that being when you crack the safe. So you woke up to a safe, you don't know the combination, and you're like one of those old fashioned safe crackers. You put your ear and you spin the knob one way until you hear a click. Then what do you do? You spin the knob the other way. You hear a click. Will you open the safe? No, you won't even try yet because you know there's going to be more than two numbers. How many numbers are there? Well, there might be three. So when you get the third click, you'll actually try to pull the lever down and open the safe, right? We call that a trial close. That's the analogy, the similarity. So imagine like you get the first three numbers lined up. What are those first three numbers? The three tens. They must love the product. They must trust and connect with you. They must trust and connect with the company that stands behind the product. Those are your first three elements that must line up because let's face it, right? If someone loves the product that you're selling but does not trust you, what will they do? Will they buy? No. They'll say, let me think about it. They won't say I don't trust you, right? They'll say let me think about it and they'll go buy the similar product, the exact product from someone they trust. We don't buy from those that we don't trust. Plain and simple. It's logic, right? You know that makes sense. It's not enough that they love your product. They also must trust and connect with you. On top of that, trust them. Not quite as important but almost as important enough to blow the sale is the fact that if they don't trust the company that either manufactured the product or is going to deliver the product or is going to be giving them customer service, that's the after the long term, that they don't trust the company that stands behind the product or that's affiliate that's going to manage their experience, they're not going to fucking buy no way. We don't buy from companies that we think will give us lousy customer service. They won't be there if there's going to be a problem. They'll sell our information to someone else. Imagine if you thought that, yeah, I like the product but I know if I give them my credit card number, they'll fucking have it up on the dark web and be selling it to a bunch of fucking criminals in the fucking East form of the East to block. Would you go, would you buy, would you do business with a company? Of course not. You would never do business in a million years with a company that acted that way. We must trust the company that we do business with. So those are your first three elements of the straight line system. The three elements that must line up before someone says yes. So imagine now, so this is why, so when we make our initial presentation, the front half of the sale ends when, when we ask for the order for the first time. So imagine using this metaphor. That's when you, you essentially have to help rapport by your intelligence gathering. You've taken control of the conversation, position yourself as an expert. So now they perceive you as an expert. You've developed tight rapport over constant on conscious level. You've made your initial presentation about the product. So you framed your airtight logical case, right? And within that, you're always mentioning the company, the brand. So if you go through script building, right? But you're always going to touch a little bit about yourself and simply you basically have by essentially by the way you carry yourself by getting into rapport, you've built up equity in that second 10. You understand by the very nature of what you do with the straight line system. When you're asked, when I say get into rapport, what am I saying? You're building the second 10. That's what's going on. So that's why you must get into rapport and at some level, like you're not because it opens them up to speak to you to gather intelligence, but remember without rapport, then you'd be like a, like a, at a very low level on your second 10. We, so no one ever buy from you. That's why people don't buy. You can't influence someone. You're out of rapport with them. You must be in rapport. That's the second 10. Now typically in very often it's not enough to close the sale, which is why people very often don't give you a lay down because the lay down would be what you ask for the order for the first time and they say, yeah, let's do it. That's a lay down sale. Now in most industries, depending on what you're saying, it's very rare. It happens three, four percent of the time. Most of the time the customer needs to hear more. Why? Because at that point in time, when you ask for the order for the first time, which marks the end of the front half of the sale, that's the end of the front half, right? Which we asked for the order. The three 10s are not high enough collectively to exceed that person's action threshold. At the end, now, now let me slow this down. At the end of the day, I think even if you think of this as just pure logic, logic is out for a second, right? When someone says yes, when they say yeah, bye, what's basically happened is their collective level of certainty for the three 10s is greater than their action threshold. If they're like me with a very low action threshold, we're like, if I'm just like in a seven, six, seven, I'm reasonably certain about the three 10s, yeah, I'll buy because I'm just not my easy person to sell to. Which is why, especially if it's a familiar product and I genuinely like the sales person, like if I am in rapport, I'm a person. I close a bit further to the, let's say if you have the straight lines in front of you, I'm a little bit to the left. I'm further towards the open. Notice I don't go down the full line. I'm easy to sell to. Not always, but typically I am because I have a low action threshold. So when you ask for the order for the first time and someone says, yeah, let's do it, what's happened is you buy your, getting it to rapport with them and through the presentation and very often because of their pre-existent, their preconceived notions about what you're selling, their knowledge of the prior, he knows about your product, right? And also because they're in massive pain, this is getting out to elements four and five, because remember when you're in massive pain, it lowers the action threshold. So sometimes when you close like that, like on a lay down, it's because the person was in massive pain and that lowered their action threshold. So the same formula still applies. In that moment because their action threshold had been lowered by their pain, the collective level of certainty after the US for the order for the first time was high enough to close the deal. That makes sense. It sounded like a mouthful, but it makes a purpose. In other words, if someone's in a lot of pain, what does that do? The pain causes their action threshold to temporarily drop. When people are in pain, their action threshold is lower. They want to get out of pain. It's pleasure. So the decision-making strategy is altered and they'll say yes, even when they're not 100% certain. So what happens is when someone's in severe pain, like my dad is very tough, not the crack, right? You begin to buy on the first, even when he's not 100% certain because he was in massive pain and that causes action threshold to drop. When he said yes, his collective level of certainty was above his action threshold in the moment. Typically, it would have not been at a high enough level of certainty, but in that moment because of pain, it was. So in other words, when you ask for the order for the first time, it's like pulling the lever of the safe down and seeing if it opens. If it opens, it means that his level of certainty collectively exceeded his current action threshold as if it was either affected by pain or it's simply his action threshold as it is normally. Might have a low one, right? So when someone buys under those circumstances in a lay down, that's what happened between the rapport that you developed during the call or the in-person meeting, between what you said to them in your initial presentation, in your intelligence gathering and their preconceived notions about your product and what they know about it, they were at a high enough level of certainty to say yes. Most of the time, though, that's not the case, which is why people then say, let me think about it, let me call you back. It's a bad time. They give you those common objections as almost like a circuit breaker, a short circuit. So it stops them from having to confront you and say, I don't trust you and I like you. I don't know enough about you. They say, oh, it sounds good when we think about it. It's a circuit breaker. So what we do then is we know that, okay, this particular safe doesn't have three numbers. We have to go for numbers four and five. You get it? When someone buys after the first call, that was a three number combination, easy safe to crack. A more difficult safe has five numbers. And sometimes it has even seven or eight. That's why you have to keep looping. That's when there's more numbers or a very difficult safe to crack. So that fourth number, again, now obviously is what? Your action threshold. So we lower someone's action threshold. That's the fourth number. And that's why when we loop back, we'll ask. So what we've done when we loop backwards, we're raising their level of certainty for each of the three 10s with our first loop. So now we're saying, because maybe it didn't open, why? It could be because they have a very high action threshold, or it could just be that you did not have them at a very high level of certainty for the first three 10s. You haven't cracked any of the first three numbers. So when the safe doesn't open it, when you ask for the order, it could be that A, they get started at a high enough level of certainty, logically or emotionally, or they get to have a very high action threshold and not feeling pain. Here's the point we're trying to make with this whole thing. And this is important here. Which one is it? Excellent. Which one? When someone doesn't buy, it could be A, that they're not certain enough about one of the three 10s, or two or three of the three 10s, or that's one possibility. Possibilities you've got them pretty certain, but their action threshold is really high. Or third, it could be that they're in absolute total denial of their pain and they have a relatively high action threshold. Even though you've gotten them very certain, you get them to a 999 on the three 10s, but there are 10 action thresholds and you haven't lowered yet. So it could be one of those things. Which one is it? Who knows? You don't know. You have no way of knowing. That's why we run these patterns the same way every time. You see, because you don't know what it really is, you're not a fucking mind reader. That's the beauty of the straight line. You do it the same way every time and we run this pattern straight down and we methodically pick the lock to the buying combination the same way a safe crack does. They go for the first number first, the second number second, the third number third. You get it that you don't, a safe crack doesn't get the fifth number first. He gets the first number, then the second, he gets the order. That's why the syntax, the order in which you go about creating certainty makes the straight line so effective because we have things in a certain order. So don't that part up. Take things in order.
